questions.question: What is Docker Hub?
options: A Docker container orchestration tool
The default public registry for Docker images
A local image build cache
A Docker networking plugin
questions.answer: The default public registry for Docker images

questions.question: Which command logs you into Docker Hub from the CLI?
options: docker auth
docker connect
docker login
docker hub login
questions.answer: docker login

questions.question: What is the full image naming convention in Docker?
options: username/repository:tag
registry/username/repository:tag
registry:tag/repository
repository/username@tag
questions.answer: registry/username/repository:tag

questions.question: Why does 'nginx:alpine' not include a username in its image name?
options: It is stored locally only
Alpine images never need a username
It is an official image maintained by Docker
The username is hidden for security
questions.answer: It is an official image maintained by Docker

questions.question: Which prefix indicates an image is hosted on GitHub Container Registry?
options: github.io/
ghcr.io/
gcr.io/
hub.github.com/
questions.answer: ghcr.io/

questions.question: What does the 'latest' tag represent in Docker image tagging?
options: The oldest available version of the image
A locked, immutable release version
The most recent build, and it is mutable
A tag reserved for base images only
questions.answer: The most recent build, and it is mutable

questions.question: In semantic versioning tags, what does the tag '1.0' typically point to?
options: The first ever build of the image
The latest patch release in the 1.0.x series
An alias for the 'latest' tag
A deprecated version of the image
questions.answer: The latest patch release in the 1.0.x series

questions.question: Which command verifies your Docker Hub username after logging in?
options: docker whoami
docker info | grep Username
docker hub status
docker config show
questions.answer: docker info | grep Username

questions.question: What does 'docker tag my-app user/my-app:1.0' do?
options: Renames the image from my-app to user/my-app:1.0
Creates a new tag user/my-app:1.0 pointing to the same image as my-app
Pushes my-app to Docker Hub under user/my-app:1.0
Deletes the original my-app image and replaces it
questions.answer: Creates a new tag user/my-app:1.0 pointing to the same image as my-app

questions.question: When you create multiple tags for the same image, what do they share?
options: Nothing; each tag creates a separate copy
The same Image ID
The same container name
The same running container instance
questions.answer: The same Image ID

questions.question: Which command pushes an image to Docker Hub?
options: docker upload user/my-app:1.0
docker push user/my-app:1.0
docker publish user/my-app:1.0
docker send user/my-app:1.0
questions.answer: docker push user/my-app:1.0

questions.question: What happens when you run 'docker run' with an image that does not exist locally?
options: Docker returns an error and exits
Docker automatically pulls the image from the registry and runs it
Docker creates an empty image with that name
Docker prompts you to build the image first
questions.answer: Docker automatically pulls the image from the registry and runs it

questions.question: Which command removes a local image?
options: docker delete
docker rm
docker rmi
docker remove-image
questions.answer: docker rmi

questions.question: What is the purpose of the '--no-cache-dir' flag when running pip install in a Dockerfile?
options: It installs packages without checking for updates
It avoids storing downloaded package files, reducing image size
It forces pip to use a specific cache directory
It disables SSL verification during downloads
questions.answer: It avoids storing downloaded package files, reducing image size

questions.question: Why should you use a specific base image tag instead of 'latest' in a Dockerfile?
options: The 'latest' tag is slower to pull
Specific tags ensure reproducible builds since 'latest' can change
The 'latest' tag is not supported by Docker Hub
Specific tags use less disk space
questions.answer: Specific tags ensure reproducible builds since 'latest' can change

questions.question: What does the Dockerfile instruction 'USER appuser' accomplish?
options: Creates a new Linux user named appuser
Sets the container to run subsequent commands as appuser instead of root
Grants appuser admin privileges in the container
Logs into Docker Hub as appuser
questions.answer: Sets the container to run subsequent commands as appuser instead of root

questions.question: Why should dependencies be installed before copying application code in a Dockerfile?
options: It is required by Docker syntax rules
It takes advantage of Docker layer caching so dependencies are not reinstalled on every code change
It ensures the application code is never included in the image
It prevents dependency conflicts
questions.answer: It takes advantage of Docker layer caching so dependencies are not reinstalled on every code change

questions.question: What does 'docker system prune -a' do?
options: Removes only stopped containers
Removes all unused images, containers, and networks
Deletes the Docker daemon configuration
Removes only dangling images
questions.answer: Removes all unused images, containers, and networks

questions.question: What does the environment variable PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 do in a Docker container?
options: Prevents Python from running compiled code
Prevents Python from writing .pyc bytecode files
Disables Python's import system
Forces Python to use interpreted mode only
questions.answer: Prevents Python from writing .pyc bytecode files

questions.question: What does the environment variable PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 do?
options: Disables Python's garbage collector
Ensures Python output is sent directly to the terminal without buffering
Prevents Python from using stdin
Limits Python's memory usage
questions.answer: Ensures Python output is sent directly to the terminal without buffering

questions.question: What does the EXPOSE instruction in a Dockerfile do?
options: Opens the port on the host machine
Documents which port the containerized application listens on
Publishes the port to the host automatically
Blocks all traffic on other ports
questions.answer: Documents which port the containerized application listens on

questions.question: How can you run two versions of the same image side by side on different ports?
options: Use the same -p port mapping for both containers
Map each container to a different host port using -p, e.g., -p 5000:5000 and -p 5001:5000
Run both containers without any port mapping
Docker does not support running two versions simultaneously
questions.answer: Map each container to a different host port using -p, e.g., -p 5000:5000 and -p 5001:5000

questions.question: What command can be used to scan a Docker image for vulnerabilities?
options: docker scan --deep
docker scout quickview
docker inspect --vulnerabilities
docker audit
questions.answer: docker scout quickview

questions.question: How can you verify that a Docker image runs as a non-root user?
options: docker ps --user
docker inspect <image> --format='{{.Config.User}}'
docker run --check-user <image>
docker image user <image>
questions.answer: docker inspect <image> --format='{{.Config.User}}'

questions.question: What is the purpose of a .dockerignore file?
options: It lists images that should not be pulled from Docker Hub
It specifies files and directories to exclude from the build context
It prevents certain containers from starting
It defines environment variables to ignore during build
questions.answer: It specifies files and directories to exclude from the build context
